---
title: "Influenza Vaccination Impact in Norway"
subtitle: "Effect of Increased Vaccination Coverage in 60+ Age Groups"
author: "Norwegian Institute of Public Health (FHI)"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  ioslides_presentation:
    widescreen: true
    smaller: false
    css: presentation.css
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
library(data.table)
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
```

## Model Overview

**Compartmental SEIR-type model** fitted to national hospitalization data

- **9 age groups**: 0–9, 10–19, 20–29, 30–39, 40–49, 50–59, 60–69, 70–79, 80+
- **Compartments**: Susceptible → Exposed → Infectious (symptomatic / asymptomatic) → Hospitalized / ICU / Recovered / Dead
- **Vaccination** modelled as protection compartments with exponential waning
- **Parameter estimation**: particle filter fitted to observed daily hospital admissions

<br>

**3 influenza seasons calibrated:** 2017/18 · 2018/19 · 2022/23


## Waning Immunity Model

Protection decays exponentially from the time of vaccination:

$$\text{VE}(t) = \text{VE}_0 \times e^{-t \,/\, T_{\text{avg}}}$$

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Average immunity duration ($T_{\text{avg}}$) | **200 days** |
| Initial VE against infection (VE₀) | **50%** |
| Initial VE against hospitalisation given infection | **20%** |

<br>

Population is distributed across discrete protection compartments (relative risk 1.0 → 0.8 in steps of 0.02), with transition rates derived analytically from $T_{\text{avg}}$.


## Scenarios

Two vaccination scenarios evaluated for each season:

| Scenario | 0–59 yrs | 60–69 | 70–79 | 80+ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Baseline** | Observed 2022/23 uptake | Observed | Observed | Observed |
| **+2% in 60+** | Unchanged | +2 pp | +2 pp | +2 pp |

<br>

Both stochastic (particle ensemble) and deterministic model runs are produced. Cost results shown are **deterministic**.


## Hospital Admissions Over Time

```{r hosp-time, out.width="95%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics("results/hosp_incidence_time.png")
```


## Hospitalisations by Age Group

```{r hosp-age, out.width="95%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics("results/hosp_by_age.png")
```


## Cumulative Outcomes

```{r cumulative, out.width="90%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics("results/cumulative_outcomes.png")
```


## Cases Averted by +2% Vaccination in 60+

```{r impact, out.width="90%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics("results/impact_plus2pct.png")
```


## Economic Impact — Deterministic Results {.smaller}

Cases averted translate to reduced costs (Baseline − +2% in 60+). Positive = averted.

```{r cost-table}
cost <- fread("results/cost_differences.csv")

cost_det <- cost[run_type == "deterministic",
  .(Season = season, Outcome = outcome,
    Baseline = Baseline, `+2% in 60+` = `+2% in 60+`,
    `Averted (Baseline − +2%)` = `Difference (averted)`)
][order(Season, Outcome)]

kable(cost_det, align = c("l", "l", "r", "r", "r"),
      booktabs = TRUE) |>
  kable_styling(font_size = 14, full_width = TRUE) |>
  column_spec(5, bold = TRUE) |>
  collapse_rows(columns = 1, valign = "top")
```


## Key Findings

- A **+2 percentage point** increase in vaccination coverage among those aged 60+ leads to a modest but consistent reduction in hospitalisations and deaths across all three seasons

- The effect is largest in seasons with high baseline severity

- **Economic savings** are driven primarily by QALY value, with smaller productivity gains (60+ are largely retired)

- Uncertainty is substantial with the current model setup — wider conclusions require more particles and sensitivity analysis on $T_{\text{avg}}$
